query: anatomy and characteristics of naso-alveolar, globulomaxillary, naso-palatine, mid-palatine cysts

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Tumors of the Palate -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: AdCC arises from the intercalated ducts of mucin-secreting glands and presents with 3 different histologic subtypes, including cribriform (most common), tubular, and solid (most aggressive). [47] See Image. Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma of the Palate. The cribriform variant presents a “Swiss-cheese” appearance, containing cylindrical pseudocysts lined with epithelial cells and hyaline material. [47] The tubular variant consists of ducts lined by 1 to 2 layers of myoepithelial-like cells. [47] The solid variant has epithelial islands with central areas of necrosis. [47] [85] The solid variant is the most aggressive of the 3 and tends to hematogenous metastasis in 40 to 60% of cases. It also tends to the perineural invasion along the palatine nerves, extending into the pterygopalatine fossa and cavernous sinus. [47] [86] [87]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Sphenopalatine Artery -- Clinical Significance -- Juvenile Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma
retrieved_docs.content: Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA) is an uncommon benign tumor that originates in the lateral wall close to the sphenopalatine foramen. This tumor predominantly affects adolescent males and is locally aggressive, often invading the surrounding structures and tissues. JNA commonly presents with painless, progressive unilateral nasal obstruction, epistaxis, and rhinorrhea. JNA has a rich vascular supply primarily from the sphenopalatine artery. Macroscopically the tumor appears as a rounded circumscribed non-encapsulated mucosa covered mass. Microscopically the tumor shows haphazardly arranged collagen with an irregular vascular pattern. Because of the rich vascular supply, the embolization of the sphenopalatine artery and surgical resection of the tumor provides a good prognosis if JNA is diagnosed early enough. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasopharynx -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The nasopharynx represents the most superior portion of the pharynx, bounded superiorly by the skull base and inferiorly by the soft palate. The nasopharynx connects the nasal cavity to the oropharynx and contains the Eustachian tube openings and adenoids. Notable clinical situations include nasopharyngeal carcinoma and adenoidal hypertrophy. This review will discuss the anatomy of the nasopharynx, including the structure, function, embryology, blood supply, innervation, anatomical variants, and clinical relevance.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxilla -- Structure and Function -- Palatine Process
retrieved_docs.content: The incisive canal, located in the anterior hard palate, connects the nasal and oral cavities and transmits the nasopalatine nerve and sphenopalatine artery. A small mucosal protrusion, the incisive papilla, overlies the opening (see Image . Inferior View of the Maxilla). [6] Anterior extension of the palatine process contributes to the formation of the anterior nasal spine.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasal Cavity -- Structure and Function -- The floor of the Nasal Cavity
retrieved_docs.content: The floor of the nasal cavity is broader than that of the roof. [1] Anterior: the palatine process of the maxilla Posterior: horizontal plate of the palatine bone

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Cysts -- Anatomical Pathology -- Branchial cleft cysts
retrieved_docs.content: The origin of branchial cleft cysts is not precisely known, but they may be from branchial cleft mucosal remnants and cystic changes in cervical chain lymph nodes; it is thought that material from the second branchial pouch leads to the formation of cysts on the anterior sternocleidomastoid in young patients, while material from the first branchial pouch leads to the formation of cysts at the auricular or mandibular sites. [53] These cysts present laterally located cystic nodules in the preauricular area, along the mandible, or anywhere from the jaw's angle to the sternocleidomastoid muscle's anterior border. [54] Lesions are typically evident in childhood or early adulthood. [55] The microscopic features of branchial cleft cysts include a stratified squamous or pseudostratified columnar epithelium, which may be ciliated, striking lymphoid follicle formation, and turbid fluid rich in cholesterol (see Image. Branchial Cyst).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose Paranasal Sinuses -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Sinus
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary sinus is a pneumatic cavity in the maxillary bone (see Image. Right and Left Maxillary Sinuses, Panoramic Radiograph). [5] It is pyramidal, with a base looking to the nasal cavity, anterior, posterior, and superior walls, and a lateral blunt apex extending into the maxillary bone's zygomatic process. The size of the maxillary sinus at the adult stage is approximately 15 mL, [6] making it the largest paranasal sinus. The anterior wall corresponds to the facial surface of the maxillary bone, with 3 identified landmarks – the canine fossa and the infraorbital foramen and groove. The infraorbital foramen is located 5 to 8 mm below the midpoint of the inferior margin of the orbit. [7] The posterior wall correlates to the maxillary tuberosity, which shapes the anterior surface of the pterygopalatine fossa. [8] The posterior wall is closely related to the contents of the pterygopalatine fossa, including the pterygopalatine ganglion and several branches of the maxillary artery, vein, and nerve. [9] Tumors and infections of the maxillary sinus and oral cavity can extend to the pterygopalatine fossa and affect these essential structures.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasopharyngeal Angiofibroma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Though histologically benign, JNA often demonstrates aggressive features with local invasion into the nasal turbinates, nasal septum, and medial pterygoid lamina. The tumor commonly extends into the nasal cavity, nasopharynx, and pterygopalatine fossa, with larger lesions involving the sphenoid, maxillary, and ethmoid sinuses. Growth may also extend through the inferior orbital fissure and into the masticator space via the infratemporal fossa. Severe disease may involve the orbit or intracranial structures, reported in approximately 10% to 37% of cases. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Palatal and Gingival Cysts of the Newborn -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Palatal cysts, including Epstein pearls and Bohn nodules, are small, painless, white to yellow, firm, non-fluctuant papules typically measuring 1 to 3 millimeters in diameter. They often appear in clusters of 2 to 6 lesions but may also present as solitary cysts. [6] Although the distinction is not clinically significant, these cysts are named based on their location. Epstein pearls are found along the mid-palatine raphe and near the junction of the hard and soft palate (see Image. Epstein Pearls). In contrast, Bohn nodules are located on the buccal and lingual surfaces of the alveolar ridges (see Image. Multiple Bohn Nodules on the Maxillary Alveolar Ridge). [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Anatomic Dead Space -- Physiologic Variants -- Maxilla
retrieved_docs.content: Variation also can occur in patients with maxillary defects or those who have undergone maxillectomy procedures. These patients have an increased anatomic dead space due to communication between the nasal and oral cavities, ultimately affecting respiratory function.

retrieved_docs.title: Palatal and Gingival Cysts of the Newborn -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Palatal development begins near the end of the eighth week of gestation. Each maxillary process forms a lateral palatine process within the oral cavity. These shelf-like structures grow horizontally from the lateral aspects of the mouth toward the midline and downward. Between the tenth and eleventh weeks of gestation (in utero), the lateral palatine processes fuse with each other, the smaller premaxillary process, and the nasal septum. Palatal fusions are usually complete by the end of the fourth month of gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasotracheal Intubation -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Hard Palate
retrieved_docs.content: The hard palate constitutes the cavity floor that runs horizontally behind the nares. The ethmoid bone, with the cribriform plate at its center, forms the ceiling of the nasal cavity. Lastly, the left and right lateral walls comprise the medial walls of the orbit superiorly and the maxillary sinus inferiorly.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nasopharynx -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: The dimensions of the nasopharynx (height and A-P diameter) vary significantly between individuals. Additionally, asymmetry of the superficial landmarks, such as the eustachian tube openings, describes the major physiologic (anatomic) variants of the nasopharynx. When asymmetry is observed on endoscopic or radiologic imaging, infiltrating tumors or mucosal masses versus benign anatomic variants should merit consideration. Lateral recesses, such as the fossa of Rosenmuller, become more visible with aging, which appears to be a consequence of physiologic degeneration of submucosal lymphoid tissue (adenoids). [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palate -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Tumors of the palate, both benign and malignant, most often present asymptomatically or are associated with a low level of discomfort.  The appearance of benign and malignant lesions of the palate is also quite similar, making it of utmost importance that the clinician be able to differentiate between them. Malignant squamous epithelial neoplasms such as verrucous carcinoma, carcinoma of the maxillary sinus, and squamous cell carcinoma may appear in the hard palate. Carcinoma of the maxillary sinus is often asymptomatic for a long time and usually presents at an advanced stage. This tumor is associated with the elderly and is most commonly diagnosed once the tumor expands to fill the sinus and produces a bulge of the alveolar ridge or palatal area on physical examination. Squamous cell carcinomas constitute more than ninety percent of all oral cancers. Non-squamous cell carcinomas of the oral cavity are rare. Risk factors for squamous cell carcinoma of the palate include alcohol, tobacco, and HPV infection. Primary squamous cell carcinoma of the soft palate is painful, causes dysphagia, and portends a much worse prognosis than tumors located more anteriorly. Verrucous carcinoma is a form of squamous cell carcinoma that rarely metastasizes, behaves indolently, and demonstrates a white papillary clinical appearance. The most common locations for verrucous carcinoma are the alveolar ridge and hard palate, and it is associated with elderly patients who wear complete denture prosthesis. Tumors of the palate can follow the palatine nerves through the greater and lesser palatine foramen and extend into surrounding bone, nerves, and soft tissue. Up to seventy percent of squamous cell carcinomas of the hard palate extends beyond the hard palate into neighboring structures. Radiographically, malignant tumors of the palate may show evidence of bone destruction and sometimes reveal a radioopacity produced by the neoplastic mass. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: During the first eight weeks of gestation, the mesenchyme condenses and forms five prominences, two mandibular, two maxillary and a frontonasal prominence. The frontonasal prominence forms the precartilagenous nasal capsule which develops as two masses around the forming nasal cavities. The maxillary prominences grow and fuse at the midline forming the lateral nasal prominences.  As the frontonasal prominence regresses and the maxillary prominence grows medially, they form the midline of the nose and philtrum of the upper lip. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Pterygopalatine Fossa -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This region is critical in transmitting parasympathetic fibers and blood vessels to and from the face and is clinically important in disease spread and cranial surgical approaches. A 3-dimensional understanding of the pterygopalatine fossa is crucial for the rhinologist and basicranial surgeon to control epistaxis and remove basicranial lesions. A pterygopalatine space approach is used to treat lesions in the areas posterior to the maxillary sinus, including the pterygopalatine space and lateral sphenoid recess. Pathologic processes in this space are rare, with the most common being juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma, neurogenic tumors, sinonasal malignancy perineural extension, and meningoencephaloceles. [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1954
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx are categorized as a sub‑type of عمليات الأنف, indicating that they fall within the broader group of nose‑related surgical interventions. These diagnostic procedures are closely linked to منظار داخلي, an internal endoscopic technique that is commonly employed to visualize and assess the nasal cavity, oral cavity, and pharyngeal structures. Understanding that diagnostic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx belong to عمليات الأنف and utilize منظار داخلي helps clinicians select appropriate endoscopic tools for accurate evaluation and management of upper airway conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior epistaxis is identified as a disorder of the nasal septum (اضطراب في الحاجز الأنفي), representing bleeding that originates from the front portion of the nasal cavity and is commonly associated with structural or mucosal abnormalities of the septum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of nasal bone is described as having the morphology of ورم خبيث أولي (primary malignant tumor). In addition, سرطانة توتية is classified as a type of ورم خبيث أولي, indicating that it too represents a primary malignant tumor. Both the nasal bone neoplasm and سرطانة توتية therefore share the underlying categorization of primary malignant tumor, reflecting their common oncologic identity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2569
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic adenocarcinoma to a lymph node of the head and neck exhibits the morphological characteristics of سرطانة غدية نقيلية, indicating that the cancerous cells in the affected lymph node display the typical features of a metastatic glandular carcinoma. This association helps pathologists recognize the tumor type and guides clinicians in diagnosing and managing metastatic disease involving head and neck lymphatic structures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1227
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head refers to any cancer that has spread to structures within the head region, and within this broader category the specific condition of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head represents a more precise manifestation. Recognizing that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the lymph nodes of the head is a subset of metastatic malignant neoplasm to the head helps clinicians assess the extent of disease spread, determine appropriate staging, and tailor management strategies for patients with head‑region metastases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.
